name: Little Signals
short_description: A calm-technology design study of six ambient notification objects that signal through air, taps, light, shadow, sound, and movement.
long_description: Little Signals is a 2022 design study from Google's Seed Studio in collaboration with London's Map Project Office, comprising six small objects (Air, Button, Movement, Rhythm, Shadow, and Tap) that deliver notifications through gentle, peripheral cues rather than screens and alerts. Building explicitly on calm-technology principles, the objects use pulses of air that stir a plant's leaves, soft taps on surfaces, rising pegs, ambient sound, and moving shadows to keep a person informed while staying at the edge of attention. It matters as physical AI because it reframes the notification layer as a distributed ecology of expressive domestic objects, trading high-attention displays for legible, low-intensity environmental signals. Google released build instructions, code, and STEP files so the objects can be reproduced with an Arduino board.

images.caption: The six Little Signals objects: Air, Button, Movement, Rhythm, Shadow, and Tap.
images.alt_text: A set of six small minimalist domestic objects arranged together, each a calm ambient notification device in soft neutral tones.

sources.title: Google's Little Signals 'design study' explores the world of ambient notifications
Little Signals
sources.access_notes: Design studio's project page documenting the collaboration.

interactions.title: Notified by your houseplant
interactions.description: The Air object sits beside a plant and releases a soft pulse of air that stirs the leaves, so a new message registers as a flutter of greenery you catch from across the room rather than a buzz in your pocket.

interactions.title: Twist for more or less
interactions.description: The Button grows taller as information accumulates and chimes softly when full; you twist its top right to pull more detail or left to dismiss, controlling how much the device tells you with a single hand gesture.

interactions.title: A glance at the pegs
interactions.description: The Movement object raises and lowers a row of seven pegs, so the shape of the bar — a single peg up versus the whole row — tells you at a glance how much is waiting without naming any of it.

interactions.title: Wave to silence the melody
interactions.description: Rhythm plays a short ambient melody whose tone carries the urgency of what arrived; if it comes at a bad moment you wave a hand over it or flip it to mute, treating a notification like a sound you can shoo away.

interactions.title: A shadow that breathes and stretches
interactions.description: The Shadow object signals by moving the shadow it casts — gently breathing when idle and stretching toward you when it senses your presence — so the cue lives on the wall and tabletop instead of on a screen.

interactions.title: Build your own from a plant and a pill bottle
interactions.description: Google released the code, STEP files, and household-materials instructions, so makers reproduce the objects on an Arduino MKR1000 — wiring Air to puff at a plant for new email, or Tap to knock on a prescription bottle as a medication reminder.

interactions.title: A household of objects sharing one alert
interactions.description: Because the six objects run the same rule-based notification logic, they could be distributed through a home so an urgent message escalates across them — Rhythm chiming in the kitchen while the Movement pegs rise in the study — grounded in their shared signaling design rather than any single screen.

interactions.title: Quieter where you are
interactions.description: With location as an input, the objects could read which room you have entered and route a signal to the nearest one — a tap on the desk while you work, the plant fluttering when you are in the living room — keeping the cue at the edge of wherever your attention already is.
